title: Seven Wonders of the Ancient World: From the Walls of Babylon to the Sewers of Rome
description:
in History |   	March 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

You may not be able to name all, or even most, of the sevôÙen wonôÙders of the ancient worl
body:
in History |   	March 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

You may not be able to name all, or even most, of the sevôÙen wonôÙders of the ancient world. But you almost cerôÙtainôÙly know that there were sevôÙen of them. In a way, that aligns well enough with the worldôÙview of the Greeks who first made refôÙerôÙence to such a list, givôÙen their near-revôÙerôÙence for that numôÙber. SevôÙen were the strings of the lyre (unless there hapôÙpened to be eight or nine), sevôÙen were the gates of Thebes, and sevôÙen were the ãwanôÙderôÙing starsã in the night sky (if you count the sun and moon). The idenôÙtiôÙty of the wonôÙders was less imporôÙtant than the length of their list, and indeed, as ancient-hisôÙtoôÙry YouTuôÙber GarôÙrett Ryan explains in his Told in Stone video above, addiôÙtions and changes were proôÙposed since the beginôÙning.
The clasôÙsic sevôÙen-wonôÙders rosôÙter includes the HangôÙing GarôÙdens of BabyôÙlon, the StatôÙue of Zeus at Olympia, the TemôÙple of Artemis at EphôÙesus, the MauôÙsoleum at HaliôÙcarôÙnasôÙsus, the ColosôÙsus of Rhodes, the LightôÙhouse of AlexanôÙdria, and the Great PyraôÙmid of Giza, that last being the only one still in exisôÙtence today.
Ryanãs alterôÙnaôÙtive list includes the EgyptôÙian labyrinth at Hawara, which Herodotus conôÙsidôÙered supeôÙriôÙor even to the PyraôÙmids; the TemôÙple of Zeus at CyzôÙiôÙcus, which Pliny the Elder described as lined by gold tubes to let in the sunôÙlight (sureôÙly stripped out as soon as the place fell into disôÙuse); the sewôÙers of Rome, a civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtionôÙal achieveôÙment unto themôÙselves; and the TheôÙater of ScauôÙrus, which, though conôÙstructôÙed out of wood for temôÙpoôÙrary use, seatôÙed an astonôÙishôÙing 80,000 peoôÙple.
Ryan comôÙpletes his sevôÙen othôÙer wonôÙders with the Altar of Horns at Delos, held in myth to have been built by ApolôÙlo himôÙself; the Walls of BabyôÙlon, which actuôÙalôÙly appear on the earôÙliôÙest known verôÙsion of the list; and, finalôÙly, the good old ColosôÙseôÙum. As over-familôÙiar (not to menôÙtion over-toured) as it may be, the FlaôÙvian AmphitheôÙater, as the ColosôÙseôÙum was known in its day, does make for a welôÙcome presôÙence among the ancient wonôÙders, being the only othôÙer one apart from the Great PyraôÙmid that we can still visôÙit today. But if you get into the mood to go marôÙvel at thauôÙmaôÙta, to borôÙrow the Greek word, by no means limôÙit yourôÙself to selecôÙtions already curatôÙed by othôÙers. The world is full of wonôÙders, and your own perôÙsonôÙal sevôÙen may not be far away.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
Watch Ancient Ruins Get Restored to their GloôÙriôÙous OrigôÙiôÙnal State with AniôÙmatôÙed GIFs: The TemôÙple of Jupiter, LuxôÙor TemôÙple & More
A WalkôÙing Tour Around the PyraôÙmids of Giza: 2 Hours in Hi Def
Ten Lost Roman WonôÙders: The Worldãs Longest TunôÙnel, Tallest Dam, Widest-SpanôÙning Bridge & More
How the Ancient Greeks Built Their MagôÙnifôÙiôÙcent TemôÙples: The Art of Ancient EngiôÙneerôÙing
Take a VirôÙtuôÙal Tour of Machu PicôÙchu, One of the New 7 WonôÙders of the World
The Ancient World Comes to Life in an AniôÙmaôÙtion FeaôÙturôÙing Istanbulãs IslamôÙic, Ottoman, Greek & ByzanôÙtine Art
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Bertrand Russellãs Advice For How (Not) to Grow Old: ãMake Your Interests Gradually Wider and More Impersonalã
description:
in Life, Philosophy |   	March 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Image by NationôÙal PorôÙtrait Gallery, via WikiôÙmeôÙdia ComôÙmons
Advice on how to grow
body:
in Life, Philosophy |   	March 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Image by NationôÙal PorôÙtrait Gallery, via WikiôÙmeôÙdia ComôÙmons
Advice on how to grow old freôÙquentôÙly comes from such banal or bloodôÙless sources that we can be forôÙgivôÙen for ignorôÙing it. PubôÙlic health offiôÙcials who disôÙpense wisôÙdom may have good intenôÙtions; pharôÙmaôÙceuôÙtiôÙcal comôÙpaôÙnies who do the same may not. In either case, the mesôÙsages arrive in a form that can bring on the despair they seek to avert. ElderôÙly peoôÙple in well-lit phoôÙtographs stroll down garôÙden paths, ballôÙroom dance, do yoga. BulôÙletôÙed lists puncôÙtuôÙatôÙed by dry citaôÙtions issue genôÙtly-wordôÙed guideôÙlines for senôÙsiôÙble livôÙing. InofôÙfenôÙsive blandôÙness as a preôÙscripôÙtion for livôÙing well.
At the othôÙer extreme are proôÙfiles of excepôÙtionôÙal casesãrelatively spry indiôÙvidôÙuôÙals who have passed the cenôÙtuôÙry mark. Rarely do their stoôÙries conôÙform to the modôÙel of abstemiousôÙness enjoined upon us by proôÙfesôÙsionôÙals. But we know that growôÙing old with digôÙniôÙty entails so much more than diet and exerôÙcise or makôÙing it to a hunôÙdred-and-two. It entails facôÙing death as squareôÙly as we face life. We need writôÙers with depth, senôÙsiôÙtivôÙiôÙty, and eloôÙquence to delivôÙer this mesôÙsage. Bertrand RusôÙsell does just that in his essay ãHow to Grow Old,ã writôÙten when the philosoôÙpher was 81 (sixôÙteen years before he evenôÙtuôÙalôÙly passed away, at age 97).
RusôÙsell does not flatôÙter his readôÙersã ratioôÙnalôÙist conôÙceits by citôÙing the latôÙest sciôÙence. ãAs regards health,ã he writes, ãI have nothôÙing useôÙful to sayãÎ. I eat and drink whatôÙevôÙer I like, and sleep when I canôÙnot keep awake.ã (We are inclined, perôÙhaps, to trust him on these grounds alone.) He opens with a driôÙly humorôÙous paraôÙgraph in which he recôÙomôÙmends, ãchoose your ancesôÙtors well,ã then he issues advice on the order of not dwelling on the past or becomôÙing a burôÙden to your chilôÙdren.
But the true kerôÙnel of his short essay, ãthe propôÙer recipe for remainôÙing young,ã he says, came to him from the examôÙple of a materôÙnal grandôÙmothôÙer, who was so absorbed in her life, ãI do not believe she ever had time to notice she was growôÙing old.ãô ãIf you have wide and keen interôÙests and activôÙiôÙties in which you can still be effecôÙtive,ã RusôÙsell writes. ãyou will have no reaôÙson to think about the mereôÙly staôÙtisôÙtiôÙcal fact of the numôÙber of years you have already lived, still less of the probôÙaôÙble shortôÙness of your future.ã
Such interôÙests, he argues, should be ãimperôÙsonôÙal,ã and it is this qualôÙiôÙty that loosens our grip. As Maria PopoôÙva puts it, ãRusôÙsell places at the heart of a fulôÙfillôÙing life the disôÙsoôÙluôÙtion of the perôÙsonôÙal ego into someôÙthing largôÙer.ã The idea is familôÙiar; in Russellãs hands it becomes a medôÙiôÙtaôÙtion on morôÙtalôÙiôÙty as ever-timeôÙly as the so-often-quotôÙed pasôÙsages from Donneãs ãMedôÙiôÙtaôÙtion XVII.ãô PhilosoôÙpher and writer John G. MesserôÙly calls Russellãs conôÙcludôÙing pasôÙsage ãone of the most beauôÙtiôÙful reflecôÙtions on death I have found in all of world litôÙerôÙaôÙture.ã
The best way to overôÙcome it [the fear of death]ãso at least it seems to meãis to make your interôÙests gradôÙuôÙalôÙly wider and more imperôÙsonôÙal, until bit by bit the walls of the ego recede, and your life becomes increasôÙingôÙly merged in the uniôÙverôÙsal life. An indiôÙvidôÙual human exisôÙtence should be like a rivôÙer: small at first, narôÙrowôÙly conôÙtained withôÙin its banks, and rushôÙing pasôÙsionôÙateôÙly past rocks and over waterôÙfalls. GradôÙuôÙalôÙly the rivôÙer grows wider, the banks recede, the waters flow more quiôÙetôÙly, and in the end, withôÙout any visôÙiôÙble break, they become merged in the sea, and painôÙlessôÙly lose their indiôÙvidôÙual being. The man who, in old age, can see his life in this way, will not sufôÙfer from the fear of death, since the things he cares for will conôÙtinôÙue. And if, with the decay of vitalôÙiôÙty, weariôÙness increasôÙes, the thought of rest will not be unwelôÙcome. I should wish to die while still at work, knowôÙing that othôÙers will carôÙry on what I can no longer do and conôÙtent in the thought that what was posôÙsiôÙble has been done.
Read Russellãs ãHow to Grow Oldã in full here. And see many more eloôÙquent medôÙiôÙtaôÙtions on aging and deathãfrom HenôÙry Miller, Andrûˋ Gide, UrsuôÙla K. Le Guin, and Grace Paleyãat The MarôÙginôÙaôÙlian.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2018.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent: ô 
Bertrand Russellãs Advice to PeoôÙple LivôÙing 1,000 Years in the Future: ãLove is Wise, Hatred is FoolôÙishã
Bertrand RusôÙsell: The EveryôÙday BenôÙeôÙfit of PhiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy Is That It Helps You Live with UncerôÙtainôÙty
Simone de Beauvoirãs PhiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy on FindôÙing MeanôÙing in Old Age
Youãre Only As Old As You Feel: HarôÙvard PsyôÙcholôÙoôÙgist Ellen Langer Shows How MenôÙtal AttiôÙtude Can PotenôÙtialôÙly Reverse the Effects of Aging
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: Roman Statues Werenãt White; They Were Once Painted in Vivid, Bright Colors
description: The idea of the clasôÙsiôÙcal periodãthe time of ancient Greece and Romeãas an eleôÙgantôÙly uniôÙfied colôÙlecôÙtion of supeôÙriôÙor aesôÙthetôÙic and
body:
The idea of the clasôÙsiôÙcal periodãthe time of ancient Greece and Romeãas an eleôÙgantôÙly uniôÙfied colôÙlecôÙtion of supeôÙriôÙor aesôÙthetôÙic and philoôÙsophôÙiôÙcal culôÙturôÙal traits has its own hisôÙtoôÙry, one that comes in large part from the era of the NeoôÙclasôÙsiôÙcal. The redisôÙcovôÙery of antiqôÙuiôÙty took some time to reach the pitch it would durôÙing the 18th cenôÙtuôÙry, when refôÙerôÙences to Greek and Latin rhetoric, archiôÙtecôÙture, and sculpôÙture were inescapable. But from the RenaisôÙsance onward, the clasôÙsiôÙcal achieved the staôÙtus of culôÙturôÙal dogôÙma.
One tenet of clasôÙsiôÙcal ideôÙalôÙism is the idea that Roman and Greek statôÙuôÙary embodôÙied an ideôÙal of pure whitenessãa misôÙconôÙcepôÙtion modôÙern sculpôÙtors perôÙpetôÙuôÙatôÙed for hunôÙdreds of years by makôÙing busts and statôÙues in polôÙished white marôÙble. But the truth is that both Greek statôÙues and their Roman counterpartsãas youãll learn in the Vox video aboveãwere origôÙiôÙnalôÙly brightôÙly paintôÙed in riotous colôÙor.
This includes the 1st cenôÙtuôÙry A.D. AugusôÙtus of PriôÙma PorôÙta, the famous figôÙure of the EmperôÙor standôÙing triôÙumphantôÙly with one hand raised. Rather than left as blank white marôÙble, the statôÙue would have had bronzed skin, brown hair, and a fire-engine red toga. ãAncient Greece and Rome were realôÙly colôÙorôÙful,ã we learn. So how did everyôÙone come to believe othôÙerôÙwise?
Itãs partôÙly an honôÙest misôÙtake. After the fall of Rome, ancient sculpôÙtures were buried or left out in the open air for hunôÙdreds of years. By the time the RenaisôÙsance began in the 1300s, their paint had fadôÙed away. As a result, the artists unearthing, and copyôÙing ancient art didnãt realôÙize how colôÙorôÙful it was supôÙposed to be. 
But white marôÙble couldnãt have become the norm withôÙout some willôÙful ignoôÙrance. Even though there was a bunch of eviôÙdence that ancient sculpôÙture was paintôÙed, artists, art hisôÙtoôÙriôÙans and the genôÙerôÙal pubôÙlic chose to disôÙreôÙgard it. WestôÙern culôÙture seemed to colôÙlecôÙtiveôÙly accept that white marôÙble was simôÙply pretôÙtiôÙer. 
White statôÙuôÙary symôÙbolôÙized a clasôÙsiôÙcal ideôÙal that ãdepends highôÙly on the greatôÙest posôÙsiôÙble deconôÙtexôÙtuôÙalôÙizaôÙtion,ã writes James I. Porter, proôÙfesôÙsor of Rhetoric and ClasôÙsics at the UniôÙverôÙsiôÙty of CalôÙiôÙforôÙnia, BerkeôÙley. ãOnly so can the valôÙues it cherôÙishôÙes be isoôÙlatôÙed: simôÙplicôÙiôÙty, tranôÙquilôÙiôÙty, balôÙanced proôÙporôÙtions, restraint, puriôÙty of formãÎ all of these are feaôÙtures that underôÙscore the timeôÙless qualôÙiôÙty of the highôÙest posôÙsiôÙble expresôÙsion of art, like a breath held indefôÙiôÙniteôÙly.ã These ideals became insepôÙaôÙraôÙble from the develôÙopôÙment of racial theôÙoôÙry.
LearnôÙing to see the past as it was requires us to put aside hisôÙtorôÙiôÙcalôÙly acquired blindôÙers. This can be exceedôÙingôÙly difôÙfiôÙcult when our ideas about the past come from hunôÙdreds of years of inherôÙitôÙed traôÙdiôÙtion, from every periôÙod of art hisôÙtoôÙry since the time of MichelanôÙgeôÙlo. But we must acknowlôÙedge this traôÙdiôÙtion as fabôÙriôÙcatôÙed. InfluôÙenôÙtial art hisôÙtoôÙriôÙan Johann Joachim WinckôÙelôÙmann, for examôÙple, extolled the valôÙue of clasôÙsiôÙcal sculpôÙture because, in his opinôÙion, ãthe whiter the body is, the more beauôÙtiôÙful it is.ã
WinckôÙelôÙmann also, Vox notes, ãwent out of his way to ignore obviôÙous eviôÙdence of colôÙored marôÙble, and there was a lot of it.ã He disôÙmissed fresôÙcoes of colôÙored statôÙuôÙary found in PomôÙpeii and judged one paintôÙed sculpôÙture disôÙcovôÙered there as ãtoo primôÙiôÙtiveã to have been made by ancient Romans. ãEviôÙdence wasnãt just ignored, some of it may have been destroyedã to enforce an ideôÙal of whiteôÙness. While many statôÙues were denudôÙed by the eleôÙments over hunôÙdreds of years, the first archaeôÙolôÙoôÙgists to disôÙcovôÙer the AugusôÙtus of PriôÙma PorôÙta in the 1860s described its colôÙor scheme in detail.
CriôÙtiques of clasôÙsiôÙcal ideôÙalôÙism donãt origôÙiôÙnate in a politôÙiôÙcalôÙly corôÙrect present. As Porter shows at length in his artiôÙcle ãWhat Is ãClasôÙsiôÙcalã About ClasôÙsiôÙcal AntiqôÙuiôÙty?,ã they date back at least to 19th cenôÙtuôÙry philosoôÙpher LudôÙwig FeuerôÙbach, who called Winckelmannãs ideas about Roman statôÙues ãan empôÙty figôÙment of the imagôÙiôÙnaôÙtion.ã But these ideas are ãfor the most part takôÙen for grantôÙed rather than quesôÙtioned,ã Porter argues, ãor else clung to for fear of losôÙing a powôÙerôÙful cachet that, even in the beleaôÙguered present, conôÙtinôÙues to transôÙlate into culôÙturôÙal presôÙtige, authorôÙiôÙty, elitôÙist satôÙisôÙfacôÙtions, and ecoôÙnomôÙic powôÙer.ã
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2019.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
Why Most Ancient CivôÙiôÙlizaôÙtions Had No Word for the ColôÙor Blue
Why Ancient Romans Paid a ForôÙtune for the ColôÙor PurôÙple ã More Than Even SilôÙver
How Ancient Greek StatôÙues RealôÙly Looked: Reseasrch Reveals Their Bold, Bright ColôÙors and PatôÙterns
The Met DigôÙiôÙtalôÙly Restores the ColôÙors of an Ancient EgyptôÙian TemôÙple, Using ProôÙjecôÙtion MapôÙping TechôÙnolôÙoôÙgy
Watch Art on Ancient Greek VasôÙes Come to Life with 21st CenôÙtuôÙry AniôÙmaôÙtion
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: 'Lettres Dûˋcoratives' Is a Celebration of Fin de Siû´cle Sign Painters' Vibrant Letterforms
description: Before digital fonts and the ability to reproduce graphics on a large scale, there were sign painters. Today, printers can spit out countless post
body:
Before digital fonts and the ability to reproduce graphics on a large scale, there were sign painters. Today, printers can spit out countless posters and ads, but there was a time when hand-painted promotional signage was needed for retail windows, and business names were often rendered just the same. Of course, itãs a trade that virtually died out with the advent of new technologies, which made it cheaper and faster to produce public messaging. In the way of LPs and film cameras, though, just because there were new methods in daily use, it certainly doesnãt mean that the art form doesnãt live on. 
A new book published by Letterform Archive, Lettres Dûˋcoratives: A Century of French Sign Paintersã Alphabets, celebrates the vivacity and timelessness of French sign painting from the 19th and early 20th centuries. Compiled from lithograph portfolios, which range from 1875 to around 1932, the volume includes more than 150 full-color reproductions of these bold lettering samples. These portfolios once served as catalogue-like albums, providing inspiration for styles and motifs that could be translated onto large billboards and small signage alike.

While decorative painting had been around long before embellished lettering came into vogue, it took a while to catch on. ãOnly in the nineteenth century did painters make alphabets into primary decorative elements,ã writes sign painter Morgane CûÇme in the introduction. ãIndeed even then, many of the letters painted on signboards, canvas awnings, and vehicles remained simple in form, following inherited models of Roman square capitals and modern variants used in printing type. The new demand for eye-catching signs called for significant change in perspective.ã
Explore more in your own copy, which you can grab from the Colossal Shop.

schema.headline: 'Lettres Dûˋcoratives' Is a Celebration of Fin de Siû´cle Sign Painters' Vibrant Letterforms

title: Grant Garmezy Molds a Full-Size Dakotaraptor from Molten Glass
description: Dakotaraptor, a fossilized skeleton of which was discovered a little more than 20 years ago by paleontologists in South Dakota, was an extremely l
body:
Dakotaraptor, a fossilized skeleton of which was discovered a little more than 20 years ago by paleontologists in South Dakota, was an extremely lethal prehistoric predator. Its feathered body, powerful legs, and huge jaw gave it an advantage as it roamed its territory some 66 million years ago. But it was really its so-called ãsickle claw,ã a huge, taloned toe that measures 9.5 inches on the outer curve. 
For artist Grant Garmezy, the ancient creature presented a unique opportunity to render a life-size sculptural version. Specializing in meticulously detailed, accurate representations of nature in glass, he took on the challenge of recreating the Dakotaraptorãs 14-foot length from snout to tail.

ãThe fossil record leaves much to interpretation, as details like feathers and posture remain a matter of scientific debate,ã says a statement from GRANADA Gallery, which supported the artist in the project. ãThis uncertainty mirrors the artistry of the project itself, where each form is a choice, a vision, and a reimagining of prehistory.ã
Go behind-the-scenes as Garmezy makes the sculpture on Instagram, and explore more of his process on YouTube.

schema.headline: Grant Garmezy Molds a Full-Size Dakotaraptor from Molten Glass

title: The Met Introduces High-Definition 3D Scans of Dozens of Art Historical Objects
description: In the age of the internet, weãre fortunate to have virtual access to museum collections around the world, thanks to objects in the public domain
body:
In the age of the internet, weãre fortunate to have virtual access to museum collections around the world, thanks to objects in the public domain and programs like The Metropolitan Museum of Artãs Open Access Initiative. Through a searchable digital catalogue, visitors to the museumãs website can see hundreds of thousands of objects, many images of which are available for download. And itãs not aloneãother institutions like the Art Institute of Chicago, The National Gallery of Art, and The Cleveland Museum of Art, among others, make pieces in their collections accessible to all.
The thing is, digital images donãt always give us the full picture, so to speak. Even two-dimensional paintings and drawings have unique textures, structural details, and materials that we can only really appreciate in person. This wonãt ever really changeãnothing beats the real thing. But one caveat is that even in person, much of the work remains hidden. We canãt see the backs of oil paintings, for example, and edges are often hidden within frames. Thanks to The Metãs continued emphasis on imaging, we can now experience every detail in three-dimensional renderings of nearly 140 significant objects in its holdings.
The Temple of Dendur (10th century B.C.E.)
The Met is home to a whopping 1.5 million historic objects, which range from the iconic Temple of Dendur and Impressionist paintings to African tribal ceremonial sculptures and medieval pottery. The museum recently published 3D models of some of these, plus numerous other objects, including nine produced in collaboration with NHK (Japan Broadcasting Corporation).
With careful attention to technical precision and color, these animated renderings are research-grade tools, allowing us to see the objects at any angle. View van Goghãs brushstrokes closer than youãre allowed to in a museum, zoom in on a Babylonian cuneiform tablet, and turn an 18th-century Turkish tile over to see its reverse side.
The Met plans to continue adding 3D scans to its online library. Explore more on the museumãs website.

schema.headline: The Met Introduces High-Definition 3D Scans of Dozens of Art Historical Objects

title: Scale the Dramatic Verticality of Grundtvigs Kirke in David Altrath's Dreamy Photos
description: The construction of Grundtvigs Kirke in Copenhagen took nearly two decades, beginning in fall of 1921 and finally reaching completion in 1940. Des
body:
The construction of Grundtvigs Kirke in Copenhagen took nearly two decades, beginning in fall of 1921 and finally reaching completion in 1940. Designed by Peder Vilhelm Jensen-Klint, it transforms the humble brick into a masterpiece of Expressionist architecture. Its pointed interior arches and vaulted ceiling, stepped crenellations, and hulking exterior nod to medieval Gothic and Romanesque styles while also exhibiting a profoundly modern sensibility.
David Altrath, a Hamburg-based photographer whose work emphasizes urban and architectural elements, captures Grundtvigsã details in an atmospheric cumulative portrait. Bathed in mellow, golden light, the churchãs pale yellow bricks appear to glow, complemented by minimal interior decor beyond some simple wooden furniture and altars.

ãThe building feels both austere and atmospheric, especially in the way light moves through the interior spaces and emphasizes the geometry of the structure,ã Altrath tells Colossal. Heãs always seeking unique relationships between form, light, and atmosphere.
The churchãs tall vertical lines and symmetry required careful attention to composition in order to capture the buildingãs architectural geometry, and the interior lighting conditions can vary depending on the weather or time of day. ãI enjoyed working with these constraints and trying to capture both the structure of the building and the quiet atmosphere of the space,ã he says.
Altrath is currently photographing modernist and contemporary architecture across Europe. See more on Instagram.

schema.headline: Scale the Dramatic Verticality of Grundtvigs Kirke in David Altrath's Dreamy Photos
